# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains framework-description evidence, implementation details, evaluation results against named baselines, ablation findings, communication-analysis measurements, and stated limitations for a multi-agent LLM software-development system.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., ChatDev, Abstract/Section 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev is presented as a chat-powered software-development framework with specialized LLM agents active in design, coding, and testing, guided by a chat chain and communicative dehallucination.

## Source Excerpt

“In this paper, we introduce ChatDev, a chat-powered software development framework in which specialized agents driven by large language models (LLMs) are guided in what to communicate (via chat chain) and how to communicate (via communicative dehallucination). These agents actively contribute to the design, coding, and testing phases through unified language-based communication.”

## Note

The statement is a framework description from the paper, not an independent external validation.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev structures work into three sequential phases—design, coding, and testing—with coding split into code writing and completion, and testing split into code review and system testing.

## Source Excerpt

“Specifically, ChatDev segments the software development process into three sequential phases: design, coding, and testing. The coding phase is further subdivided into subtasks of code writing and completion, and the testing phase is segmented into code review (static testing) and system testing (dynamic testing).”

## Note

This evidence describes the workflow decomposition used by the system.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each subtask is handled by two specialized agents, one instructor and one assistant, who communicate in multi-turn dialogue until consensus is reached.

## Source Excerpt

“In every subtask, two agents, each with their own specialized roles ... perform the functions of an instructor and an assistant... They engage in a multi-turn dialogue, working cooperatively until they achieve consensus, extracting solutions... ultimately leading to the completion of the subtask.”

## Note

This is a structural property of the system’s subtask execution.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1 Memory
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev uses short-term memory for dialogue continuity within a phase and long-term memory for carrying prior-phase solutions across phases.

## Source Excerpt

“...we accordingly segment the agents’ context memories based on their sequential phases, resulting in two functionally distinct types of memory: short-term memory and long-term memory. Short-term memory is utilized to sustain the continuity of the dialogue within a single phase, while long-term memory is leveraged to preserve contextual awareness across different phases.”

## Note

This evidence states the memory scheme used to manage context across the workflow.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1 Memory
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The system passes only subtask solutions, not full dialogue history, between phases to reduce information overload and preserve cross-phase continuity.

## Source Excerpt

“By sharing only the solutions of each subtask rather than the entire communication history, ChatDev minimizes the risk of being overwhelmed by too much information, enhancing concentration on each task and encouraging more targeted cooperation, while simultaneously facilitating cross-phase context continuity.”

## Note

This is a stated rationale for the long-term memory design.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Communicative dehallucination makes the assistant ask for more specific information before giving a final response, using a temporary role reversal.

## Source Excerpt

“In contrast, our communicative dehallucination mechanism features a deliberate ‘role reversal’, where the assistant takes on an instructor-like role, proactively seeking more specific information ... before delivering a conclusive response. After the instructor provides a specific modification suggestion, the assistant proceeds to perform precise optimization.”

## Note

This evidence states the mechanism used to address coding hallucinations.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Section 4 Metrics
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The evaluation defines Quality as the product of completeness, executability, and consistency.

## Source Excerpt

“Quality is a comprehensive metric that integrates various factors to assess the overall quality of software, quantified by multiplying completeness, executability, and consistency.”

## Note

This defines how the paper’s aggregate quality score is computed.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 4 Implementation Details
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The implementation used 5 subtasks across 3 phases, assigned roles including CEO, CTO, programmer, reviewer, and tester, and ran on ChatGPT-3.5 at temperature 0.2 with Python 3.11.4 feedback.

## Source Excerpt

“We divided software development into 5 subtasks within 3 phases, assigning specific roles like CEO, CTO, programmer, reviewer, and tester... We used ChatGPT-3.5 with a temperature of 0.2 and integrated Python-3.11.4 for feedback.”

## Note

This is a concrete implementation configuration used in the reported experiments.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 4 Implementation Details
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A subtask ended after either two unchanged code modifications or 10 rounds of communication, and communicative dehallucination was activated during code completion, review, and testing.

## Source Excerpt

“A subtask would terminate and get a conclusion either after two unchanged code modifications or after 10 rounds of communication. During the code completion, review, and testing, a communicative dehallucination is activated.”

## Note

This evidence captures stopping conditions and where the dehallucination mechanism was applied.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In Table 1, ChatDev scores 0.5600 completeness, 0.8800 executability, 0.8021 consistency, and 0.3953 quality.

## Source Excerpt

“ChatDev 0.5600 0.8800 0.8021 0.3953” in Table 1 under “Completeness Executability Consistency Quality.”

## Note

These are the paper’s reported aggregate performance numbers for ChatDev.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In Table 1, ChatDev’s quality score of 0.3953 exceeds GPT-Engineer’s 0.1419 and MetaGPT’s 0.1523.

## Source Excerpt

“GPT-Engineer ... Quality 0.1419”; “MetaGPT ... Quality 0.1523”; “ChatDev ... Quality 0.3953.” The table note states “† indicates significant statistical differences (p ≤ 0.05) between a baseline and ours.”

## Note

The source reports higher quality for ChatDev relative to both baselines in this evaluation table.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In pairwise evaluation against GPT-Engineer, ChatDev wins 77.08% of GPT-4 judgments and 90.16% of human judgments.

## Source Excerpt

“GPT-Engineer GPT-4 22.50% 77.08% 00.42%” and “Human 09.18% 90.16% 00.66%” in Table 2 under “Baseline Wins / ChatDev Wins / Draw.”

## Note

These are pairwise preference outcomes reported by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In pairwise evaluation against MetaGPT, ChatDev wins 57.08% of GPT-4 judgments and 88.00% of human judgments.

## Source Excerpt

“MetaGPT GPT-4 37.50% 57.08% 05.42%” and “Human 07.92% 88.00% 04.08%” in Table 2 under “Baseline Wins / ChatDev Wins / Draw.”

## Note

These are pairwise preference outcomes reported by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Removing communicative dehallucination lowers reported performance from 0.5600/0.8800/0.8021/0.3953 to 0.4700/0.8400/0.7983/0.3094 on completeness, executability, consistency, and quality.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 4, “ChatDev 0.5600 0.8800 0.8021 0.3953” while “╲ CDH 0.4700 0.8400 0.7983 0.3094.” The text adds: “eliminating communicative dehallucination results in a decrease across all metrics.”

## Note

This is ablation evidence that the mechanism matters within the paper’s setup, but it is bounded to the authors’ experimental design and does not establish generality beyond it.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Section 4.3 Communication Analysis
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The communication breakdown reported in Figure 3 assigns 57.20% of agent communication to natural language.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 3 depicts a communication breakdown, with natural language at 57.20%.”

## Note

This is a reported descriptive measurement of communication composition.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: incident
citation: Qian et al., Section 4.3 and Limitations
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper reports recurring code-generation failures such as “Method Not Implemented” in review (34.85% of discussions) and “ModuleNotFound” in testing (45.76% of errors), and states the system is currently more suitable for prototype systems than complex real-world applications.

## Source Excerpt

“Particularly, the ‘Method Not Implemented’ issue is most common in communication between reviewers and programmers during code reviews, accounting for 34.85% of discussions.”
“The most frequent error is ‘ModuleNotFound’ (45.76%), followed by ‘NameError’ and ‘ImportError’ (each at 15.25%).”
“Currently, these technologies are more suitable for prototype systems rather than complex real-world applications.”

## Note

These observations attach concrete failure patterns and an explicit scope limitation to the reported system, creating tension with broader autonomy claims.
---

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains framework-description evidence, implementation details, evaluation results against named baselines, ablation findings, communication-analysis measurements, and stated limitations for a multi-agent LLM software-development system.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., ChatDev, Abstract/Section 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high

contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev is presented as a chat-powered software-development framework with specialized LLM agents active in design, coding, and testing, guided by a chat chain and communicative dehallucination.

## Source Excerpt

“In this paper, we introduce ChatDev, a chat-powered software development framework in which specialized agents driven by large language models (LLMs) are guided in what to communicate (via chat chain) and how to communicate (via communicative dehallucination). These agents actively contribute to the design, coding, and testing phases through unified language-based communication.”

## Note

The statement is a framework description from the paper, not an independent external validation.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev structures work into three sequential phases—design, coding, and testing—with coding split into code writing and completion, and testing split into code review and system testing.

## Source Excerpt

“Specifically, ChatDev segments the software development process into three sequential phases: design, coding, and testing. The coding phase is further subdivided into subtasks of code writing and completion, and the testing phase is segmented into code review (static testing) and system testing (dynamic testing).”

## Note

This evidence describes the workflow decomposition used by the system.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each subtask is handled by two specialized agents, one instructor and one assistant, who communicate in multi-turn dialogue until consensus is reached.

## Source Excerpt

“In every subtask, two agents, each with their own specialized roles ... perform the functions of an instructor and an assistant... They engage in a multi-turn dialogue, working cooperatively until they achieve consensus, extracting solutions... ultimately leading to the completion of the subtask.”

## Note

This is a structural property of the system’s subtask execution.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1 Memory
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
ChatDev uses short-term memory for dialogue continuity within a phase and long-term memory for carrying prior-phase solutions across phases.

## Source Excerpt

“...we accordingly segment the agents’ context memories based on their sequential phases, resulting in two functionally distinct types of memory: short-term memory and long-term memory. Short-term memory is utilized to sustain the continuity of the dialogue within a single phase, while long-term memory is leveraged to preserve contextual awareness across different phases.”

## Note

This evidence states the memory scheme used to manage context across the workflow.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: claim-support
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.1 Memory
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The system passes only subtask solutions, not full dialogue history, between phases to reduce information overload and preserve cross-phase continuity.

## Source Excerpt

“By sharing only the solutions of each subtask rather than the entire communication history, ChatDev minimizes the risk of being overwhelmed by too much information, enhancing concentration on each task and encouraging more targeted cooperation, while simultaneously facilitating cross-phase context continuity.”

## Note

This is a stated rationale for the long-term memory design.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: method
citation: Qian et al., Section 3.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Communicative dehallucination makes the assistant ask for more specific information before giving a final response, using a temporary role reversal.

## Source Excerpt

“In contrast, our communicative dehallucination mechanism features a deliberate ‘role reversal’, where the assistant takes on an instructor-like role, proactively seeking more specific information ... before delivering a conclusive response. After the instructor provides a specific modification suggestion, the assistant proceeds to perform precise optimization.”

## Note

This evidence states the mechanism used to address coding hallucinations.
```

```markdown
---

evidence_id: evidence-07

contradiction_relevance: none
---
The evaluation defines Quality as the product of completeness, executability, and consistency.

## Source Excerpt

“Quality is a comprehensive metric that integrates various factors to assess the overall quality of software, quantified by multiplying completeness, executability, and consistency.”

## Note

This defines how the paper’s aggregate quality score is computed.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1

evidence_id: evidence-08

mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The implementation used 5 subtasks across 3 phases, assigned roles including CEO, CTO, programmer, reviewer, and tester, and ran on ChatGPT-3.5 at temperature 0.2 with Python 3.11.4 feedback.

## Source Excerpt

“We divided software development into 5 subtasks within 3 phases, assigning specific roles like CEO, CTO, programmer, reviewer, and tester... We used ChatGPT-3.5 with a temperature of 0.2 and integrated Python-3.11.4 for feedback.”

## Note

This is a concrete implementation configuration used in the reported experiments.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-09

citation: Qian et al., Section 4 Implementation Details
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
A subtask ended after either two unchanged code modifications or 10 rounds of communication, and communicative dehallucination was activated during code completion, review, and testing.

## Source Excerpt

“A subtask would terminate and get a conclusion either after two unchanged code modifications or after 10 rounds of communication. During the code completion, review, and testing, a communicative dehallucination is activated.”

## Note

This evidence captures stopping conditions and where the dehallucination mechanism was applied.
```

“ChatDev 0.5600 0.8800 0.8021 0.3953” in Table 1 under “Completeness Executability Consistency Quality.”

```markdown
---

“GPT-Engineer ... Quality 0.1419”; “MetaGPT ... Quality 0.1523”; “ChatDev ... Quality 0.3953.” The table note states “† indicates significant statistical differences (p ≤ 0.05) between a baseline and ours.”

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-12

source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In pairwise evaluation against GPT-Engineer, ChatDev wins 77.08% of GPT-4 judgments and 90.16% of human judgments.

## Source Excerpt

“GPT-Engineer GPT-4 22.50% 77.08% 00.42%” and “Human 09.18% 90.16% 00.66%” in Table 2 under “Baseline Wins / ChatDev Wins / Draw.”

```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
In pairwise evaluation against MetaGPT, ChatDev wins 57.08% of GPT-4 judgments and 88.00% of human judgments.

## Source Excerpt

“MetaGPT GPT-4 37.50% 57.08% 05.42%” and “Human 07.92% 88.00% 04.08%” in Table 2 under “Baseline Wins / ChatDev Wins / Draw.”

## Note

These are pairwise preference outcomes reported by the paper.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: metric
citation: Qian et al., Table 4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Removing communicative dehallucination lowers reported performance from 0.5600/0.8800/0.8021/0.3953 to 0.4700/0.8400/0.7983/0.3094 on completeness, executability, consistency, and quality.

## Source Excerpt

In Table 4, “ChatDev 0.5600 0.8800 0.8021 0.3953” while “╲ CDH 0.4700 0.8400 0.7983 0.3094.” The text adds: “eliminating communicative dehallucination results in a decrease across all metrics.”

## Note

This is ablation evidence that the mechanism matters within the paper’s setup, but it is bounded to the authors’ experimental design and does not establish generality beyond it.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---

article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-15

source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The communication breakdown reported in Figure 3 assigns 57.20% of agent communication to natural language.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 3 depicts a communication breakdown, with natural language at 57.20%.”

## Note

This is a reported descriptive measurement of communication composition.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-04-026
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: incident
citation: Qian et al., Section 4.3 and Limitations
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07924
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The paper reports recurring code-generation failures such as “Method Not Implemented” in review (34.85% of discussions) and “ModuleNotFound” in testing (45.76% of errors), and states the system is currently more suitable for prototype systems than complex real-world applications.

## Source Excerpt

“Particularly, the ‘Method Not Implemented’ issue is most common in communication between reviewers and programmers during code reviews, accounting for 34.85% of discussions.”  
“The most frequent error is ‘ModuleNotFound’ (45.76%), followed by ‘NameError’ and ‘ImportError’ (each at 15.25%).”  
“Currently, these technologies are more suitable for prototype systems rather than complex real-world applications.”

## Note

These observations attach concrete failure patterns and an explicit scope limitation to the reported system, creating tension with broader autonomy claims.
---
